name: Italy
summary: Italy ranges from cool northern regions to warm Mediterranean south, with major regional diversity and many important indigenous grapes.

alto-adige-trentino-friuli.name: Alto Adige / Trentino / Friuli-Venezia Giulia
alto-adige-trentino-friuli.summary: Cooler north-eastern Italian zone influenced by altitude and mountains, producing fresh aromatic whites and elegant reds.
alto-adige-trentino-friuli.styleSummary: Fresh aromatic whites and some light to medium-bodied reds, often with good acidity.
examTips: Think cooler north-eastern Italy with freshness and acidity.
Altitude and Alpine influence are key themes.
Pinot Grigio is especially important here.

Pinot Grigio.summary: Fresh light to medium-bodied white grape producing pear and citrus styles in cooler sites.
Pinot Grigio.style: Light to medium-bodied white with pear, citrus and subtle stone fruit notes.
examTips: Usually fresher and more precise here than in hotter regions.

Sauvignon Blanc.summary: Aromatic white grape producing citrus, herb and green fruit notes with good freshness.
Sauvignon Blanc.style: Fresh aromatic whites with citrus, herb and green fruit notes.
winemaking: Usually stainless steel to preserve aromatics
examTips: Think freshness and aromatic purity.

Chardonnay.summary: Versatile white grape showing fresh citrus and stone fruit, sometimes with subtle oak.
Chardonnay.style: Fresh citrus to stone-fruit styles, sometimes with subtle oak.
examTips: Usually fresher and lighter than richer warm-climate Chardonnay.

veneto.name: Veneto
veneto.summary: Large north-eastern Italian region producing a wide range of styles including reds, Soave whites and Pinot Grigio.
veneto.styleSummary: Wide range including reds, Soave whites and Pinot Grigio.
examTips: Veneto is diverse, so think style by grape and subregion.
Corvina is key for Valpolicella styles.
Garganega is the main grape of Soave.

Corvina.summary: Key red grape of Veneto, giving red cherry fruit, freshness and medium body, especially in Valpolicella styles.
Corvina.style: Red cherry, medium body and fresh acidity in Valpolicella styles.
winemaking: Fresh styles or appassimento-based richer styles
examTips: Think red cherry and freshness in basic styles.
Also important in appassimento-based richer wines.

Garganega.summary: Main white grape of Soave, producing medium-bodied citrus and almond-accented wines.
Garganega.style: Medium-bodied white with citrus, pear and almond notes.
winemaking: Can be simple or more textured
examTips: Remember Soave = Garganega.

Pinot Grigio.summary: Widely planted white grape usually made in a light fresh pear-and-citrus style.
Pinot Grigio.style: Light to medium-bodied white with pear and citrus notes.
examTips: Usually light, fresh and straightforward rather than highly aromatic.

provence.name: Provence
provence.summary: Warm Mediterranean region strongly associated with pale dry rosé, plus some reds.
provence.styleSummary: Warm-climate rosé and red wines with ripe fruit profile.
examTips: Provence is primarily a rosé region in exam context.
Think pale colour, dry style and ripe but fresh fruit.

Grenache.summary: Warm-climate grape contributing ripe red fruit, body and alcohol, especially useful in rosé blends.
Grenache.style: Ripe red fruit and body, often important in rosé blends.
winemaking: Often used in rosé and red blends
examTips: Important in Provence rosé blends.

Mourvedre.summary: Late-ripening grape adding dark fruit, spice and structure in warm coastal blends.
examTips: Usually gives more structure than Grenache.

tuscany.name: Tuscany
tuscany.summary: Classic central Italian region centred on Sangiovese, producing savoury reds with freshness and regional variation.
tuscany.styleSummary: Sangiovese-led reds with freshness and savoury character.
examTips: Sangiovese is the core grape of Tuscany.
Think sour cherry, herbs and savoury structure.
Altitude and hillside sites help preserve freshness.

Sangiovese.summary: Italy's key central red grape, known for sour cherry fruit, savoury notes and fresh acidity.
Sangiovese.style: Sour cherry, herbs, savoury notes with fresh acidity.
viticulture: Needs sufficient warmth but not excessive heat
examTips: High acidity plus sour cherry is a key clue.
Usually more savoury than plush.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: International variety used in richer structured Tuscan styles and blends.
Cabernet Sauvignon.style: Black fruit, cassis and firmer structure in international styles.
examTips: Used in more international-style Tuscan wines.

Merlot.summary: Plum-fruited softer-textured international grape used in Tuscan blends and varietal wines.
Merlot.style: Plum, black fruit and rounder texture.
examTips: Usually softer and rounder than Cabernet Sauvignon.

marche.name: Marche
marche.summary: Adriatic-facing central Italian region best known for fresh, characterful Verdicchio.
marche.styleSummary: Fresh whites, especially Verdicchio, with some elegant reds.
examTips: Verdicchio is the key grape here.
Think freshness, citrus and almond note.

Verdicchio.summary: Fresh central Italian white grape producing citrus, green apple and almond-accented wines with good acidity.
Verdicchio.style: Fresh citrus, green apple and sometimes almond notes, with good acidity.
viticulture: Performs well in Marche hillside sites
examTips: Almond note is a useful clue.
Usually fresher and more characterful than neutral Italian whites.

umbria.name: Umbria
umbria.summary: Inland central Italian region producing traditional whites and some structured reds from hilly sites.
umbria.styleSummary: Whites based around local and traditional grapes, plus some quality reds.
examTips: Think inland central Italy with traditional white blends.
Grechetto is the most useful grape to remember.

Grechetto.summary: Medium-bodied Umbrian white grape with citrus, orchard fruit and some texture.
Grechetto.style: Medium-bodied white with citrus, orchard fruit and some texture.
winemaking: Usually blended or varietal dry whites
examTips: More textural and characterful than very neutral whites.

Trebbiano.summary: Neutral productive white grape often used in blends for light dry wines.
Trebbiano.style: Neutral to lightly citrus white, often used in blends.
winemaking: Often used in simple dry styles
examTips: Usually more neutral than Grechetto.

lazio.name: Lazio
lazio.summary: Central Italian region near Rome known mainly for traditional white blends.
lazio.styleSummary: Mainly white wines from traditional local blends.
examTips: Think simple to moderate white wines from traditional blends.
Volcanic soils can be relevant in some zones.

Malvasia.summary: Traditional aromatic to semi-aromatic white grape often used in Lazio blends.
Malvasia.style: Aromatic to neutral white, often medium-bodied with soft fruit notes.
examTips: Usually softer and broader than crisp high-acid whites.

Trebbiano.summary: Widely planted neutral white grape used for light dry blends.
Trebbiano.style: Light, neutral to citrus white used widely in blends.
examTips: Think neutral supporting grape rather than aromatic star.

abruzzo.name: Abruzzo
abruzzo.summary: Central Adriatic region known for Montepulciano-based reds with ripe fruit and generous body.
abruzzo.styleSummary: Important for Montepulciano-based reds with ripe fruit and good body.
examTips: Montepulciano is the key grape here.
Do not confuse the grape Montepulciano with Vino Nobile di Montepulciano in Tuscany.

Montepulciano.summary: Deep-coloured Italian red grape producing ripe black fruit, body and moderate to firm tannin.
Montepulciano.style: Deep colour, ripe black fruit and medium to high tannin.
viticulture: Performs well in warm central Italy
winemaking: Usually fruit-driven but can be more structured
examTips: Dark fruit and deeper colour are useful clues.
Name confusion is a common exam trap.

campania.name: Campania
campania.summary: Southern Italian region influenced by volcanic soils, producing structured whites and powerful reds.
campania.styleSummary: Structured whites and full-bodied reds influenced by volcanic soils.
examTips: Volcanic soils are a key theme in Campania.
Aglianico is one of Italy’s most structured red grapes.
Southern whites can still show high acidity due to altitude.

Aglianico.summary: Powerful late-ripening red grape producing high tannin, high acidity wines with ageing potential.
Aglianico.style: Dark fruit, high tannin, high acidity, structured and ageworthy.
examTips: High tannin + high acidity is key.
Often compared to Nebbiolo in structure.

Fiano.summary: Textural white grape producing complex wines with floral, nutty and honeyed notes.
Fiano.style: Medium to full-bodied white with floral, honey and nutty notes.
examTips: More texture and weight than many Italian whites.

Greco.summary: Fresh high-acid white grape producing citrus-driven wines with mineral edge.
Greco.style: Fresh citrus white with high acidity and mineral character.
examTips: Higher acidity than many southern Italian whites.

puglia.name: Puglia
puglia.summary: Hot southern Italian region producing ripe, full-bodied reds, often from high-yielding vineyards.
puglia.styleSummary: Ripe, full-bodied reds with soft acidity.
examTips: Think hot climate and ripe fruit.
Often softer acidity and higher alcohol.
Primitivo is genetically related to Zinfandel.

Primitivo.summary: Early-ripening grape producing high-alcohol, ripe fruit-driven wines.
Primitivo.style: Ripe black fruit, jammy character and high alcohol.
examTips: Think jammy fruit and high alcohol.
Compare with Zinfandel.

Negroamaro.summary: Dark-fruited grape producing medium to full-bodied wines with moderate tannin.
Negroamaro.style: Dark fruit, moderate tannin and slightly bitter finish.
examTips: Often slightly bitter finish is a clue.

basilicata.name: Basilicata
basilicata.summary: Small southern region known for structured Aglianico from elevated volcanic sites.
basilicata.styleSummary: Structured reds from Aglianico with freshness from elevation.
examTips: Aglianico from altitude is the key idea.
Think structure, tannin and ageing potential.

Aglianico.summary: Powerful structured red grape producing dark-fruited, high-acid, high-tannin wines with ageing potential.
Aglianico.style: Dark fruit, high tannin and high acidity with ageworthy potential.
examTips: Think southern Italy but with freshness from altitude.
Very ageworthy style.

sicily.name: Sicily
sicily.summary: Large Mediterranean island producing ripe wines, with cooler volcanic sites on Mount Etna.
sicily.styleSummary: Ripe wines with regional variation, especially cooler styles on Etna.
examTips: Sicily combines hot climate with cooler volcanic zones.
Etna is key for fresher, more elegant styles.
Nero d’Avola is the main grape to remember.

Nero d’Avola.summary: Main Sicilian red grape producing ripe dark fruit, spice and full-bodied wines.
Nero d’Avola.style: Ripe dark fruit, spice and medium to full body.
examTips: Think ripe fruit and warm climate.
Usually softer than northern Italian reds.

Nerello Mascalese.summary: Volcanic-site red grape producing elegant wines with higher acidity and lighter colour.
Nerello Mascalese.style: Lighter colour, higher acidity, red fruit and mineral notes.
winemaking: Often compared to Pinot Noir or Nebbiolo styles
examTips: Think Etna and higher elevation.
More elegant than Nero d’Avola.

Grillo.summary: Fresh to aromatic white grape used for both dry wines and historically Marsala.
Grillo.style: Fresh citrus and tropical fruit white.
examTips: Often fresher than expected for a hot region.
